attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department accompanied by her husband. Over the past month, she has become increasingly withdrawn and depressed, expressing thoughts of worthlessness and guilt. Her mood brightens temporarily in response to positive events. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears withdrawn but cooperative. Her vital signs are within normal limits. She reports sleeping 12-14 hours per day and has gained 5 kg due to increased appetite. Her husband notes she has been writing in her diary during the night but has no recollection of this the next day. She describes a heavy, leaden feeling in her arms and legs. She has a long-standing pattern of being highly sensitive to rejection, which has led her to avoid social contact. She denies alcohol or drug use.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Major depressive disorder with atypical features
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features characteristic of atypical depression, a subtype of major depressive disorder. Major depressive disorder with atypical features is distinguished by mood reactivity (temporary improvement in mood in response to positive events) plus at least two of: hypersomnia, increased appetite or weight gain, leaden paralysis (heavy feeling in limbs), and interpersonal rejection sensitivity. This patient exhibits all of these features.

Bipolar II disorder would require a history of hypomanic episodes, which are not described in this case. The nocturnal diary writing without recall could suggest a hypomanic episode, but more history would be needed to establish this diagnosis.

Schizoaffective disorder requires both psychotic symptoms and mood symptoms to be present. While the patient has mood symptoms, there is no evidence of delusions, hallucinations, or thought disorder.

Persistent depressive disorder (formerly dysthymia) is characterized by chronic, low-grade depressive symptoms lasting at least two years. This patient's symptoms are more acute and severe.

Somatic symptom disorder involves excessive thoughts, feelings, or behaviours related to somatic symptoms. While the patient reports leaden paralysis, this is a characteristic feature of atypical depression rather than a primary somatic focus.

The presence of parasomnia (sleep-writing) is noteworthy and should prompt consideration of medication effects or other sleep disorders, though it does not affect the primary diagnosis in this case. Treatment typically begins with selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs), though monoamine oxidase inhibitors (MAOIs) have historically shown particular efficacy in atypical depression.
learningPoints: Major depressive disorder with atypical features requires mood reactivity plus at least two of: hypersomnia, hyperphagia/weight gain, leaden paralysis, or interpersonal rejection sensitivity.
Parasomnia behaviours like sleep-writing should prompt consideration of other diagnoses or medication effects.
Atypical depression often responds better to monoamine oxidase inhibitors than other antidepressants, though SSRIs are typically first-line.
references: American Psychiatric Association. (2013). Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (5th ed.). Arlington, VA: Author.
Thase ME. Recognition and diagnosis of atypical depression. J Clin Psychiatry. 2007;68 Suppl 8:11-6.  Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/17640153/
